pull.title: Run feature_bind_port_(discover|externalip).py in CI
pull.body:
`feature_bind_port_discover.py` and `feature_bind_port_externalip.py` require a routable address on the machine to run. Since that was not predictably available on CI, those tests required a manual setting up of IP addresses (e.g. using `ifconfig`) and then running the tests with a command line option telling them that the addresses are set up. The tests were not run in CI and [got rot](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/31293#issuecomment-2497792487).

Change that to auto-detect, from the tests, whether the needed IP addresses are present and if yes, run the test, otherwise skip it. Also change the CI to configure the needed addresses when running the functional tests. This way the tests will be run regularly on CI.

Fixes: https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/31336

events.body: The following sections might be updated with supplementary metadata relevant to reviewers and maintainers. ### Code Coverage & Benchmarks For details see: https://corecheck.dev/bitcoin/bitcoin/pulls/33362. ### Reviews See [the guideline](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#code-review) for information on the review process. | Type | Reviewers | | ---- | --------- | | ACK | [willcl-ark](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/33362#pullrequestreview-4197463689), [frankomosh](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/33362#pullrequestreview-4234395872), [ryanofsky](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/33362#pullrequestreview-4320887198) | If your review is incorrectly listed, please copy-paste <!--meta-tag:bot-skip--> into the comment that the bot should ignore. ### Conflicts Reviewers, this pull request conflicts with the following ones: * [#31723](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/31723) (qa: Facilitate debugging bitcoind inside functional tests by hodlinator) If you consider this pull request important, please also help to review the conflicting pull requests. Ideally, start with the one that should be merged first.

`f047fce8ab...9b30b0bea8`: set up the needed addresses of the VMs outside of the VMs, when setting them up using `docker run` and `docker network connect`, instead of from inside the VM. The former seems to be simpler. Idea from the [discussion above](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/33362#discussion_r2340540732).
Moved to draft, given this builds on #32757, and review should go there.

`70558c66068c977df5673adeca4d68a80a3d853f...60c708c8d3dc976ab9a50339638a6a2e70868a54`: rebase due to conflicts, now that https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32757 is merged, remove it from this PR.
Just one comment and a refactor patch to try and remove the duplicated code for your perusal.

verification.payload:
tree e128f293d298d97858d0dde2bc0ea62b28cf9996
parent 58dccd27e11de68f810145fc30631a2c446f3bf5
author Vasil Dimov <vd@FreeBSD.org> 1757423542 +0200
committer Vasil Dimov <vd@FreeBSD.org> 1777465121 +0200

test: make feature_bind_port_(discover|externalip).py auto-detect the skip condition

Instead of requiring a run with an explicit `--ihave1111and2222`, detect
whether the routable addresses are set up and if not, then skip the test.

To detect whether the addresses are set use `bitcoind` - start it
and ask it to bind on them and see if it will error with
"Unable to bind". Since this is what the tests do anyway, just start
the nodes and see if an exception will be raised like
`FailedToStartError` / "Unable to bind".

This makes it possible for the CI to run
`feature_bind_port_discover.py` and
`feature_bind_port_externalip.py` by just setting up the
addresses, without having to explicitly provide `--ihave1111and2222`.

Co-authored-by: willcl-ark <will@256k1.dev>

author Vasil Dimov <vd@FreeBSD.org> 1757597954 +0200
committer Vasil Dimov <vd@FreeBSD.org> 1777465205 +0200

ci: add one more routable address to the VMs (docker containers)

Also explicitly specify which addresses from the docker network to
assign to the VM.

With `1.1.1.5` and `1111:1111::5` set on the machine, the tests
`feature_bind_port_discover.py` and
`feature_bind_port_externalip.py` will run.

events.body:
ACK 75cf9708a05d08c3de5dffd91228444d942254b8

Looks like CI may be stuck, so I guess we have to see why that may be when it concludes. But code looks good to me

Tested ACK 75cf9708a05d08c3de5dffd91228444d942254b8. Built from source.

Reviewed `cleanup_partially_started_nodes()` against the `node.running` lifecycle in `test_node.py`, logic seems good. Address constants are consistent across `02_run_container.py` and both test files.
Tested on Linux x86_64.
Code review ACK 75cf9708a05d08c3de5dffd91228444d942254b8. Tested locally with and without the special addresses, and the detection seems to work well.

A side-effect of this change is that if you're running the CI (locally), and a job fails, you now cannot run any another job, until removing the failed container (this wasn't previously the case, you could run other job, just not the same job, without removing the failed container):
```bash
+ docker image prune --force --filter label=bitcoin-ci-test
Emulate Docker CLI using podman. Create /etc/containers/nodocker to quiet msg.
+ docker run --rm --interactive --detach --tty --cap-add=LINUX_IMMUTABLE --mount=type=bind,src=/root/ci_scratch,dst=/root/ci_scratch,readonly '--mount=type=volume,src=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind_ccache,dst=/ci_container_base/ci/scratch_ ₿🧪_/ccache' --mount=type=volume,src=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind_depends,dst=/ci_container_base/depends/built --mount=type=volume,src=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind_depends_sources,dst=/ci_container_base/depends/sources --mount=type=volume,src=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind_previous_releases,dst=/ci_container_base/prev_releases --env-file=/tmp/env-root-ci_native_fuzz_valgrind --name=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind --network=ci-ip6net --ip6=1111:1111::5 --platform=linux ci_native_fuzz_valgrind
Emulate Docker CLI using podman. Create /etc/containers/nodocker to quiet msg.
Error: IPAM error: requested ip address 1111:1111::5 is already allocated to container ID 7b93d8ab1dc9ba118608cd992b1757b57cb6a3e2fc21eb8b613453b26dff71ba
Command '['docker', 'run', '--rm', '--interactive', '--detach', '--tty', '--cap-add=LINUX_IMMUTABLE', '--mount=type=bind,src=/root/ci_scratch,dst=/root/ci_scratch,readonly', '--mount=type=volume,src=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind_ccache,dst=/ci_container_base/ci/scratch_ ₿🧪_/ccache', '--mount=type=volume,src=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind_depends,dst=/ci_container_base/depends/built', '--mount=type=volume,src=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind_depends_sources,dst=/ci_container_base/depends/sources', '--mount=type=volume,src=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind_previous_releases,dst=/ci_container_base/prev_releases', '--env-file=/tmp/env-root-ci_native_fuzz_valgrind', '--name=ci_native_fuzz_valgrind', '--network=ci-ip6net', '--ip6=1111:1111::5', '--platform=linux', 'ci_native_fuzz_valgrind']' returned non-zero exit status 126.
```

> A side-effect of this change is that if you're running the CI (locally), and a job fails, you now cannot run any another job, until removing the failed container (this wasn't previously the case, you could run other job, just not the same job, without removing the failed container)

It seems like this problem is actually worse than described, because now you can no longer run jobs simultaneously on the same host. The script sets up a global network, but after 75cf9708a05d08c3de5dffd91228444d942254b8 instead of containers just having dynamic IP addresses, they now have conflicting hardcoded static ip addresses, so can't be started at the same time. Errors look like: `Error: IPAM error: requested ip address 1111:1111::5 is already allocated to container ID 0dc...`

It looks like it might be possible to fix the failed job problem by moving [`container kill`](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/10dfdd4b9fab578e43ba211325967cbc28cb6652/ci/test/02_run_container.py#L190) command into a try/finally block. Using [`RESTART_CI_DOCKER_BEFORE_RUN`](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/10dfdd4b9fab578e43ba211325967cbc28cb6652/ci/test/02_run_container.py#L120) could also be a workaround.

I think to fix the simultaneous job problem, the script might need to be updated to use different networks for different containers instead of the same network for every container. This might make sense anyway since CI containers shouldn't be communicating with each other.

   # shellcheck disable=SC2086

+                  --cap-add NET_ADMIN \
comments.body:
The privileges only apply to the guest, not to host. The guest ("container" in docker language) has no way to manipulate the host's networking.

https://docs.docker.com/engine/containers/run/#container-networking
https://docs.docker.com/engine/network/

> We use our own `--network` in CI ...

Hmm. Maybe that could be an easier way to add the dummy routable addresses. Then they will be available for the whole duration of the entire CI run, not only during the functional tests as in this PR. I guess this should be ok, will look into this...

         ).stdout.strip()
         os.environ["CI_CONTAINER_ID"] = container_id
 
+        run(["docker", "network", "connect", "--ip=1.1.1.5", "ci-ip4net", container_id])
comments.body:
The container has been attached to both the default bridge network and `ci-ip4net`, hence two 
IPv4 addresses. Do you think `getifaddrs()` may return the bridge IP first, and cause `Discover()` to find the wrong address?

If thats the case then I think it might be worth it to disconnect from bridge after connecting to `ci-ip4net`.

comments.body:
Using `rg -C 2 "self.start_nodes()" .` it does not seem like any other tests do this (surround with try/except) so I am wondering is this cleanup even necessary?

In case it is, is it only for this test or for all tests? In case you can't argue why it is necessary this should be removed in both files, in case it is only for these tests adding a helper is not worth it imo. in case its relevant for all tests this should be a separate PR (but you can leave the duplicated logic in this PR)

comments.body:
`feature_bind_port_externalip.py` fails if I remove `node.has_explicit_bind = True`:

```
2026-04-10T11:11:30.108371Z TestFramework (ERROR): Unexpected exception:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../test/functional/test_framework/test_framework.py", line 143, in main
    self.run_test()
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^
  File ".../test/functional/feature_bind_port_externalip.py", line 95, in run_test
    assert_equal(local['port'], expected_port)
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../test/functional/test_framework/util.py", line 83, in assert_equal
    raise AssertionError("not(%s)" % " == ".join(str(arg) for arg in (thing1, thing2) + args))
AssertionError: not(11826 == 30001)
```

This is because for node0 without `node.has_explicit_bind = True` the framework adds `Command-line arg: bind="0.0.0.0:11826"` which overrides the test's own `Command-line arg: port="30001"`.

-            default=False)

+        self.setup_nodes()
 
-    def skip_test_if_missing_module(self):
-        if not self.options.ihave1111:
-            raise SkipTest(
-                f"To run this test make sure that {ADDR} (a routable address) is assigned "
-                "to one of the interfaces on this machine and rerun with --ihave1111")
+    def setup_nodes(self):
+        self.add_nodes(self.num_nodes, self.extra_args)
+        # TestNode.start() will add -bind= to extra_args if has_explicit_bind is
+        # False. We do not want any -bind= thus set has_explicit_bind to True.
+        for node in self.nodes:
+            node.has_explicit_bind = True
comments.body:
Did you mean `feature_bind_port_discover.py`? That test passes if `node.has_explicit_bind = True` is removed, but that would cripple the test, making it to test less cases. The test has this for node0 and node1:

```
['-discover', f'-port={self.bind_ports[0]}', '-listen=1'], # Without any -bind
['-discover', f'-bind=0.0.0.0:{self.bind_ports[1]}'], # Explicit -bind=0.0.0.0
```
If `node.has_explicit_bind = True` is removed then the first one, node0, also gets `Command-line arg: bind="0.0.0.0:11894"` which is then the same as for node1. In other words, then node0 and node1 will test the same case - with explicit `-bind=0.0.0.0` and "without any `-bind`" will not be tested.

comments.body:
Yes sorry there were two errors in my reasoning: 1) yes I meant `feature_bind_port_discover.py` not the file I commented on. 2) I thought that since Discover now properly runs on explicit wildcard binds it is not required to add `node.has_explicit_bind = True` anymore.

As far as I understand it now it is still required as a hatch so the testing framework does not inject/override the bind...which seems like an issue with the framework but you are right that `node.has_explicit_bind = True` can not be removed.

edit: actually I might meant both files I can honestly not remember anymore but it does not matter.

diff --git a/test/functional/test_framework/test_framework.py b/test/functional/test_framework/test_framework.py
index b809e68f4df..8cc7121f102 100755
--- a/test/functional/test_framework/test_framework.py
+++ b/test/functional/test_framework/test_framework.py
@@ -535,6 +535,27 @@ class BitcoinTestFramework(metaclass=BitcoinTestMetaClass):
             # Wait for nodes to stop
             node.wait_until_stopped()

+    def cleanup_partially_started_nodes(self):
+        """Tear down nodes left running after a failed start_nodes().
+
+        After start_nodes() raises (e.g. FailedToStartError), some nodes may be
+        RPC-connected, some may have a live process without RPC, and some may
+        already have exited. Stop the connected ones cleanly and force-kill the
+        rest so the framework's teardown can proceed.
+        """
+        for node in self.nodes:
+            if not node.running:
+                continue
+            if node.rpc_connected:
+                node.stop_node(wait=node.rpc_timeout)
+            else:
+                node.process.kill()
+                node.process.wait(timeout=node.rpc_timeout)
+                node.process = None
+                node.stdout.close()
+                node.stderr.close()
+                node.running = False
+
     def restart_node(self, i, extra_args=None, clear_addrman=False, *, expected_stderr=''):
         """Stop and start a test node"""
         self.stop_node(i, expected_stderr=expected_stderr)
```

I replaced `raise e` with `raise`, as in python3 this gets you a fuller traceback, but can drop that part if you don't think it's necessary

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -535,6 +535,27 @@ def stop_nodes(self, wait=0):
             # Wait for nodes to stop
             node.wait_until_stopped()
 
+    def cleanup_partially_started_nodes(self):
+        """Tear down nodes left running after a failed start_nodes().
+
+        After start_nodes() raises (e.g. FailedToStartError), some nodes may be
+        RPC-connected, some may have a live process without RPC, and some may
+        already have exited. Stop the connected ones cleanly and force-kill the
+        rest so the framework's teardown can proceed.
+        """
+        for node in self.nodes:
+            if not node.running:
+                continue
+            if node.rpc_connected:
+                node.stop_node(wait=node.rpc_timeout)
+            else:
+                node.process.kill()
+                node.process.wait(timeout=node.rpc_timeout)
comments.body:
In commit "test: make feature_bind_port_(discover|externalip).py auto-detect the skip condition" (1b93983bf5c8460b2e5741f5efe88c0adbed4a79)

Note this is using `rpc_timeout` for a wait which isn't not an RPC. This is reasonable since `rpc_timeout` is used in similar ways elsewhere, but might be something to clean up later. (I've suggested renaming `rpc_timeout` to `failure_timeout` let it be used as a general timeout for how long a test is allowed to be idle before it fails.)

In commit "test: make feature_bind_port_(discover|externalip).py auto-detect the skip condition" (1b93983bf5c8460b2e5741f5efe88c0adbed4a79)

I think this comment should say **why** this test needs to avoid `-bind` arguments. I understand vaguely the it helps with coverage but it would be good to know a more specific reason. I think comment should also mention the `node.has_explicit_bind = True` setting. I don't understand why `has_explicit_bind` is only needed now but not needed previously. It seems like it should have always been added?

(Separately, these option names both seem misleading. `bind_to_localhost_only` seems like it should be called `bind_to_localhost` since it does bind to localhost but doesn't actually prevent other binds. The `has_explicit_bind` name is also wrong if goal of setting the option is to test the node behavior when there are no `-bind` arguments. Seems better to call something like `bind_to_unique_ports` or `bind_auto` and invert it, setting it true by default.
